title: Two-stage hazard-plus-calibration stack with sigmoid-only final mapping
summary: Recent F1 pit-stop work uses a cascaded setup that predicts both next-lap stop and horizon-to-stop, while survival literature treats this naturally as a discrete-time hazard with time-varying covariates.
rationale: Recent F1 pit-stop work uses a cascaded setup that predicts both next-lap stop and horizon-to-stop, while survival literature treats this naturally as a discrete-time hazard with time-varying covariates. For this leaderboard, the practical angle is a two-stage stack: one model for immediate hazard, one for remaining-laps risk, then a monotone final combiner. Calibration docs matter because isotonic can change AUC via ties, while sigmoid preserves ranking.
implementation_hint: Build a secondary target such as laps-until-next-pit within a driver-race sequence, fit a discrete-time hazard or survival-style auxiliary model using person-period framing or a horizon classifier, and feed its OOF hazard/remaining-laps predictions into the main binary model. If post-processing is tested, prefer Platt/sigmoid on held-out folds rather than isotonic so ROC ordering is preserved.
expected_effect: Adds a structured notion of imminent vs eventual pit pressure, which may sharpen predictions in medium-probability regions without rewriting the whole pipeline.
risk: Auxiliary labels must be generated strictly causally and resets around pit stops must be correct; poorly fit calibration or isotonic ties can lower AUC even if probabilities look nicer.
